title: Polio case and death rates in the United States
subtitle: The reported rates are per 100,000 US population and include both [wild-](#dod:wild-poliovirus) and [vaccine-derived poliovirus](#dod:vaccine-derived-poliovirus) infections that occurred indigenously and as imported cases.
caption:
Polio case and death rates in the United States
The reported rates are per 100,000 US population and include both wild- and vaccine-derived poliovirus infections that occurred indigenously and as imported cases.

This line chart tracks the reported polio case and death rates in the United States from 1910 to 2023, with the vertical axis measuring the number of cases or deaths per 100,000 people. The data includes both wild- and vaccine-derived poliovirus infections that occurred indigenously and as imported cases. Two distinct lines trace the trends: a brown line representing the polio case rate and a blue line representing the polio death rate. For the first half of the 20th century, the case rate fluctuates significantly, showing periodic spikes. A sharp increase begins in the early 1940s, culminating in the chart's highest peak around 1952, where the case rate reaches approximately 36 per 100,000. Following this peak, the case rate drops precipitously, falling below 5 per 100,000 by 1960 and reaching zero shortly thereafter, where it remains for the rest of the timeline. The death rate follows a similar but much lower trajectory, staying consistently below 5 per 100,000 throughout the entire period. It shows a small corresponding peak around 1952, reaching roughly 2 per 100,000, before also declining to zero by the early 1960s. From 1965 to 2023, both lines remain flat at the zero baseline, indicating no reported cases or deaths per 100,000 population during this span. The visual pattern shows a transition from variable, episodic outbreaks to a sustained absence of reported polio cases and deaths.
